methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $232M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $6.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Redwood Materials
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series D), post-money $6B+

data.narrative:
Lunar Energy, a Series D climate and energy company founded by Kunal Girotra, has raised $232M to date. Valuation analysis relies on two triangulation methods applied against a peer universe of 4 same-sector, same/adjacent-stage private companies (Redwood Materials at $6.0B, KoBold Metals at $2.0B, Electric Hydrogen at $1.0B, and Amogy at $700M), supplemented by reference to 1 public comparable and 8 additional private peers in the climate-tech space.

Stage-progression methodology, applying a 1.5x step-up multiplier typical of Series D advancement, implies a valuation of $348M. Peer-median analysis across the 4-company comparable set yields $1.5B, with observed range spanning $700M to $6.0B. The peer-median method carries significant weight given sector maturity and data availability, though the wide dispersion ($700M–$6.0B) reflects heterogeneity in technology maturity and market positioning.

Triangulated analysis supports a range of $296M (low) to $1.7B (high), with a median of $924M. The stage-progression method anchors the lower bound; peer-median anchors the upper. Key uncertainties include the appropriate multiplier for Series D-stage climate companies in this subcategory, degree of technology differentiation relative to peers, and capital efficiency relative to the sample. The $1.5B peer median may overweight Redwood's exceptional valuation; excluding it would substantially lower the peer-driven estimate.
